prompt: Hi there! I've got a bit of a logistics puzzle and my brain has completely given up on it, so I'm hoping you can think it through with me. We're running a small tournament with 16 teams. Each match takes 45 minutes including changeover, we have 2 courts available, and the venue is ours from 9:00 to 18:00 with a mandatory one-hour lunch break at 12:00 where both courts close. A single-elimination bracket for 16 teams needs 15 matches total. Can we actually fit the whole thing in one day? Please walk through the math step by step rather than just giving me a yes or no, because I need to defend this schedule to the venue owner.

Okay so this might be a silly question but it's been bugging me all week. My phone plan gives me 40GB of data per month. I use roughly 800MB on a normal weekday and about 2GB on weekend days because I watch videos. This month has 22 weekdays and 8 weekend days. Am I going to run out before the month ends, and if so, around which day? Show me how you're calculating it because I always mess up somewhere with the units.
Three friends split a dinner bill. Ari pays twice what Ben pays. Cleo pays 10 more than Ben. The total is 110. How much does each person pay? Show the equations.

I'd really appreciate your thinking on this one because everyone I ask gives me a different answer. I've been offered two payment structures for a 6-month freelance contract. Option A is a flat 4,000 per month. Option B is 2,500 per month plus 15% of any revenue my work generates above a 10,000 monthly baseline, and the client's honest projection is that monthly revenue will be around 18,000 but could realistically land anywhere between 12,000 and 25,000. Which option has the higher expected value, where's the break-even point, and — separate question — is expected value even the right lens here for a freelancer who needs to make rent every month? Take it step by step please.

A water tank holds 1,200 liters. Pipe A fills it at 40 L/min. Pipe B drains it at 25 L/min. If the tank starts at 300 liters and both pipes are open, how long until it's full, and what would happen instead if the tank started empty and pipe A only ran for the first 20 minutes?

So my partner and I are trying to figure out whether it makes sense to buy a chest freezer, and we keep going in circles, so let me just dump the numbers on you. The freezer costs 250 and uses about 1 kWh per day, electricity is 0.15 per kWh. Buying meat and vegetables in bulk would save us roughly 60 per month on groceries, but realistically we'd only keep it well-stocked maybe 8 months of the year because we travel. There's also the fact that our garage hits 35°C in summer which apparently makes freezers work harder, though I have no idea how to quantify that. Ignoring the summer thing or making a reasonable assumption about it, how many months until the freezer pays for itself, and is there anything in my framing you'd push back on?

If all Bloops are Razzies and no Razzies are Lazzies, and some Lazzies are Wazzies, which of the following must be true, might be true, or cannot be true? (1) Some Bloops are Lazzies. (2) No Bloops are Lazzies. (3) Some Wazzies are not Razzies. Explain each briefly.

Honestly not sure if this is a math question or a life question, but here goes. I commute 48km round trip, 5 days a week, about 46 working weeks a year. My current car does 12 km per liter and fuel is 1.10 per liter. A used hybrid I'm eyeing does 22 km per liter but would cost me 9,000 after selling my current car, and insurance would go up by 300 a year. Purely on fuel savings minus the insurance bump, how many years until the switch pays off, and at what fuel price would the payback drop under 5 years? I have a feeling the answer is going to disappoint me but I want to see the actual numbers laid out.

A conference has 5 talks (A, B, C, D, E) and 2 rooms running in parallel over 3 time slots. Constraints: A and B share a speaker so they can't be in the same slot. C must come before E (any earlier slot). D must be in slot 2. Produce one valid schedule and state whether the solution is unique.
